_meta.title: India fiscal federalism — center-to-state flows
_meta.units: ₹ thousand crore (₹ '000 cr) at current prices

fc_periods.name: 13th FC
fc_periods.note: Last year of 13th FC. Center retained 68% of divisible pool.

fc_periods.name: 14th FC
fc_periods.note: Big jump — vertical share raised from 32% to 42%. States got more untied money but lost some grant flexibility.

fc_periods.name: 15th FC
fc_periods.note: Cut by 1pp to absorb the J&K/Ladakh restructuring into Union UTs.

sources: Finance Commission XIV & XV reports (horizontal shares)
RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian States (GSDP, own tax)
_meta.notes: Per-state figures are approximations within ±10% for headline metrics; FC shares are exact. 'Contribution' is an estimated federal-tax footprint (income tax, corporate tax, GST/IGST origin, customs) — methodology is necessarily approximate because most central tax incidence is destination-blind.

pros: Largest GSDP — ~₹38 lakh crore in FY24, ~14% of national output.
Mumbai = financial capital: RBI, SEBI, BSE/NSE, all big banks HQ'd here.
Highest own-tax mobilisation in absolute terms; least dependent on Center for revenue.
cons: Severe net donor — pays ~₹4 to the Center for every ₹1 it gets back as devolution.
Sharp intra-state inequality: Mumbai-Pune corridor pulls in capital, Vidarbha/Marathwada stagnate.
Urban primacy comes with brittle infrastructure — Mumbai monsoon flooding, suburban rail at 250% capacity.

pros: Most industrialised state — auto, leather, textiles, electronics manufacturing clusters.
Highest own-tax/GSDP ratio among large states (~7.5%); strong VAT/GST collection machinery.
Best public services delivery: health, mid-day meals, women's literacy lead national averages.
cons: Net donor: contributes ~3× what it receives as devolution.
FC15 horizontal share barely moved despite stronger fiscal effort — penalises performance.
Water-stressed; Chennai's 2019 'Day Zero' showed urban planning hasn't kept up with growth.

pros: Ease-of-business leader: cluster manufacturing (chemicals, pharma, gems), GIFT City IFSC.
Per-capita income among top 5 — diversified economy beyond just Surat/Ahmedabad.
Lowest fiscal deficit consistently — Gujarat model of conservative state finances.
cons: Net donor by a wide margin; refining and chemical exports route central taxes via Mumbai/Delhi attribution.
Human development indices lag economic numbers — child malnutrition, female labour participation weak.
Vulnerable to oil shocks and global trade disruptions given export dependence.

pros: Bengaluru = global IT/IT-services hub: ~40% of India's tech exports originate here.
Strong own-tax base; emerging as biotech and ER&D services capital.
Highest startup density per capita in India; Karnataka model of urban innovation.
cons: FC15 cut horizontal share from 4.71% → 3.65% — punished for fast growth in Bengaluru's tax base.
Sharpest deviation between contribution and devolution: ~₹4 in vs ~₹1 out (after FC15 cut).
Bengaluru's infrastructure collapsing under growth: traffic, water, lakes — civic capacity not scaled.

pros: Largest FC horizontal share — gets ~₹2 lakh crore/year of central tax devolution.
Demographic weight: 17% of India's population gives it political and fiscal heft.
Recent infrastructure push (expressways, Jewar airport, defence corridor) starting to show results.
cons: Net recipient by far — devolution + grants exceed own tax revenue most years.
Lowest per-capita GSDP among large states; ~half the national average.
Tax administration capacity weak; large informal sector limits own-revenue mobilisation.

pros: Strategic gateway to Northeast and to Bangladesh/Bhutan trade.
Diverse economy: tea, jute, manufacturing in Haldia, IT in Salt Lake/Rajarhat.
FC15 share rose slightly — recognising population needs over economic dynamism.
cons: Deindustrialisation since the 1970s — Kolkata's manufacturing base hollowed out.
Heavy debt burden; high revenue deficit constrains capital spending.
Political friction with Center periodically delays GST compensation and central scheme funds.

pros: Second-largest FC horizontal share — strong recognition of need and population.
Highest GSDP growth rate among large states 2018-2023 (~10% nominal).
Young population (median age 22) — potential demographic dividend if education+jobs follow.
cons: Per-capita income lowest among large states; ~one-third of national average.
Massive outmigration of working-age men; remittances visible in GSDP but mask weak local economy.
Tax base extremely thin — own tax/GSDP ratio under 4%; nearly entirely dependent on Center.

pros: Renewable energy leader — largest solar capacity in India; vast Thar suitable for utility-scale.
Diversified base: minerals (zinc, marble), tourism, agriculture (mustard, rapeseed).
FC15 share rose ~0.5pp — recognising larger geography and forest cover.
cons: Water-stressed; agriculture-heavy economy faces climate exposure.
Net recipient — devolution + grants exceed own-tax revenue most years.
High freight burden — landlocked, far from ports adds to manufacturing cost.

pros: Long coastline (~970 km) — ports (Visakhapatnam, Krishnapatnam) anchor logistics.
Pharma cluster in Visakhapatnam; second-largest in India after Hyderabad.
Strong own-tax effort — agricultural tax administration relatively effective.
cons: Lost Hyderabad to Telangana in 2014 bifurcation — capital city construction at Amaravati stalled, then restarted.
Special-category status promised in 2014 but never granted — perennial center-state grievance.
FC15 cut horizontal share slightly despite bifurcation hardship narrative.

pros: Highest human development index in India — health, literacy, gender outcomes lead.
Remittance economy — Gulf inflows ~30% of GSDP; cushions external shocks.
Public-finance literacy of population is high — fiscal debates are part of mainstream politics.
cons: Sharpest FC15 cut — share dropped 2.50% → 1.93% (~23% reduction) for performing on demography and growth.
Aging population, low fertility rate — declining demographic dividend.
Limited manufacturing — GSDP heavily services + remittances, vulnerable to Gulf labour-policy shifts.

pros: Hyderabad pharma + IT corridor: second-largest tech employer city after Bengaluru.
Newest large state (formed 2014) — built modern administrative infrastructure from scratch.
Per-capita income among top 5 states despite young statehood.
cons: FC15 cut share by ~14% — fast growth penalised in horizontal formula.
Heavily dependent on Hyderabad metro for ~50% of GSDP; concentration risk.
Rising public debt — state borrowings stretched for irrigation (Kaleshwaram) and welfare schemes.

pros: Third-largest FC horizontal share — strong recognition of size and need.
Soybean, wheat, pulses heartland — agricultural GSDP one of highest in India.
FC15 share rose — recognising forest cover (extra weight) and demographic needs.
cons: Per-capita income well below national average; large rural+tribal population.
Manufacturing growth slow — Indore is bright spot but state-wide industrial base thin.
Net recipient — devolution exceeds own tax revenue nearly every year.

pros: Mineral-rich (iron ore, bauxite, coal) — royalty receipts steady; aluminium hub.
Disaster-management capacity built post-1999 cyclone is best-in-class globally.
Strong fiscal discipline under one-party rule for 24 years (until 2024).
cons: Resource-curse risk — mining receipts can mask weak diversification.
Per-capita income below national average despite resource wealth.
Coastal vulnerability — Bay of Bengal cyclones intensifying with climate change.

pros: Granary of India — 22% of national wheat, 11% of rice; food-security backbone.
Per-capita income above national average; strong rural Punjabi economy plus diaspora.
FC15 share rose moderately — partial recognition of stagnation.
cons: Highest debt-to-GSDP ratio among large states (~47%) — fiscal stress is structural.
Free electricity to agriculture has wrecked groundwater; ecological + fiscal crisis.
Industrial economy stagnant since 1990s — Ludhiana hosiery cluster losing ground to Tirupur.

pros: Gurugram = NCR financial-services and IT hub; auto manufacturing in Manesar/Faridabad.
Highest per-capita income among large states (~₹3.0 lakh in FY24).
Strong own-tax effort — fiscal capacity matches its small size.
cons: FC horizontal share at ~1.1% is dwarfed by what it sends as contribution.
Severe net donor: ~5–6× contribution vs devolution.
Air pollution + Yamuna depletion; NCR environmental costs externalised.

pros: Steel + coal — 20% of India's steel production; mineral royalties cushion finances.
Forest cover ~44% — eligible for FC's forest-weight component.
FC15 share rose ~0.3pp — recognising forest and demographic-need parameters.
cons: Maoist insurgency in south Bastar persists — security costs depress development spending.
Heavy mining + thermal dependence — environmental and just-transition risk.
Per-capita income below national average; tribal population (~30%) historically marginalised.

pros: Resource-rich: 28% of India's coal, large iron ore + uranium reserves.
Strategic location — supplies raw material to steel belts in Odisha/WB/Chhattisgarh.
Forest cover ~30% gets weight in FC formula.
cons: Per-capita income among lowest in India; mineral wealth doesn't trickle to local population.
Tribal land rights vs mining lease conflicts unresolved — recurring agitations.
Tax administration weak; informal economy dominates.

pros: Tea + petroleum (Digboi is India's oldest refinery) — historic revenue streams.
Gateway to NE — strategic location for India's Act East policy.
Special-category state benefits — 90:10 funding ratio for centrally sponsored schemes.
cons: Floods every year — Brahmaputra inundates ~⅓ of state, recurring fiscal drain.
Per-capita income below national average; industrial base limited.
FC15 cut share slightly — controversial given climate vulnerability.

pros: Highest per-capita income among hill states; pharma/auto belt around Haridwar-Rudrapur.
Hydropower potential + tourism (Char Dham, Jim Corbett, Rishikesh).
Strong literacy, gender outcomes — small population, manageable governance unit.
cons: Ecologically fragile — Himalayan disasters (2013 Kedarnath, 2021 Chamoli, 2023 Joshimath) recurring.
Out-migration from hill districts — 'ghost villages' phenomenon, agriculture abandoned.
Heavily dependent on tourism + centrally funded hydro projects.

pros: Highest forest cover proportion + hydropower capacity per capita.
Special-category state — 90:10 central funding; near-universal literacy.
FC15 share rose meaningfully — forest weight is increasingly load-bearing.
cons: Highest per-capita debt among states; pension burden growing fast.
Climate/cloudburst events (2023 floods cost ~₹12,000 cr) hit fiscal stability.
Limited industrial base — apples + tourism dominate; little tax-revenue diversification.

pros: Highest per-capita income in India (~₹4.5 lakh in FY24).
Tourism + mining + IT services + pharma — diversified for size.
Small population means per-capita central spend is high.
cons: Mining ban + COVID hit tourism — twin shocks exposed economic narrowness.
Net donor relative to own-tax capacity; but tiny in absolute terms.
Environmental degradation — coastal erosion, illegal mining residue, over-tourism.

pros: Highest tribal literacy in NE; strong public-services delivery for size.
Bamboo, rubber, agri-tech — niche but growing.
Special-category state benefits; 90:10 funding.
cons: Landlocked + bordered by Bangladesh on three sides — connectivity bottleneck.
Heavy dependence on Center; own tax revenue under 3% of GSDP.
Small market — manufacturing scale economics don't work.

pros: Highest rainfall on earth (Mawsynram) — hydropower + ecological tourism potential.
FC15 share rose ~0.13pp — recognises forest cover and population needs.
Special-category state — 90:10 funding.
cons: Mining moratorium (rat-hole coal mining) constrained main revenue stream since 2014.
Per-capita income low; limited manufacturing; topographic constraints on agriculture.
Connectivity weak — airport upgrades have lagged.

pros: Strategic — borders Myanmar, gateway for Act East trade.
Sericulture, organic farming, sports excellence (boxing, weightlifting).
FC15 share rose — recognising NE special needs.
cons: Ethnic conflict (Meitei-Kuki crisis since May 2023) has devastated economy and fiscal flows.
Heavy dependence on Center; own revenue under 2% of GSDP.
Insurgency-related shutdowns periodically halt economic activity.

pros: Highest tribal literacy + English fluency among NE states.
Cultural tourism (Hornbill Festival) — niche but visible.
FC15 share rose — recognising small population's per-capita needs.
cons: Lowest own-tax/GSDP ratio in India (under 1.5%).
Insurgency + Naga Accord pending for decades — political uncertainty.
Limited industrial base; bamboo + niche agriculture only.

pros: Highest literacy in NE (~91%); strong public health.
Bordering Myanmar + Bangladesh — Act East and Kaladan project corridor.
Cleanest state by several rankings — low pollution baseline.
cons: Tiny tax base; nearly all expenditure financed by Center.
Mountainous terrain — limited arable land; food largely imported.
Recent Myanmar refugee inflows strain local services and fiscal capacity.

pros: Highest FC15 share boost (+0.39pp) — largest area, sparse population, huge forest weight.
Hydropower potential — 50,000+ MW theoretical capacity.
Strategic LAC frontier — central capital expenditure on roads, airports growing fast.
cons: Lowest population density; service delivery extremely expensive per head.
Border with China is contested — security/diplomatic considerations limit civilian use of some land.
Connectivity to mainland still poor; many districts road-cut for months in monsoon.

pros: First fully organic state in India (2016) — premium agri-export brand.
Highest per-capita GSDP among NE states; pharma cluster pre-2024.
Cleanest, lowest-corruption rankings consistently.
cons: Tiny population means absolute fiscal numbers are small; concentration risk.
GSDP heavily dependent on pharma units that may relocate post tax-holiday expiry.
2023 Teesta-III dam disaster + repeated GLOFs are climate-financial risk.

pros: Highest per-capita income among states/UTs (~₹4.6 lakh).
Service-economy + central-government footprint generate huge tax base.
Strong public-services investment (mohalla clinics, public schools) per capita.
cons: As UT, gets no Finance Commission devolution — depends on central grants & own revenue.
Center-state friction (UT vs state powers) tied up in Supreme Court for years.
Air pollution + groundwater depletion — environmental costs unrecouped.

pros: Strategic location; large central capital expenditure on roads, tunnels (Zojila, Banihal).
Tourism + horticulture (apples, saffron) — uniquely high-value agri sector.
Pre-2019 had special status with own constitution — high HDI for the region.
cons: Reorganised as UT in 2019 — lost FC devolution; now financed via Union grants entirely.
Conflict overhang + central control depresses private investment.
Connectivity disrupted by terrain + weather; tourism volatile to security conditions.
